learn.title: Offshore Wind Farm Design
learn.intro: Offshore wind farms harness the powerful, consistent winds over oceans to generate clean electricity. Their design, installation, and grid connection are pivotal for meeting global decarbonization goals, offering higher capacity factors, lower visual impact, and significant scale potential that land‑based farms cannot match.
learn.c1_text: Turbines sit on monopile or jacket foundations anchored deep in the sea. A typical 14‑MW farm spreads 30 turbines 700‑meter apart, creating a 4‑km² footprint—like arranging giant wind farms as a coordinated dance that maximizes energy capture.
learn.c2_text: Installation uses specialized offshore vessels, modular components, and GPS‑guided cranes. A 14‑MW array can be commissioned in 18 months with 12 vessels, illustrating the logistical choreography required to bring wind to the sea.
learn.c3_text: Export cables carry 14‑MW to shore, interfacing with high‑voltage grids. India’s 1.5‑GW project will use a 400 kV link, demonstrating how offshore farms integrate into national grids, boosting reliability and reducing carbon intensity.
learn.stat1: Global offshore wind capacity reached 59 GW in 2023
learn.stat2: Offshore turbines average 4.5‑MW capacity, higher than land‑based
learn.stat3: Offshore wind has a capacity factor of ~45%
learn.stat4: India aims for 5 GW offshore by 2030

learn.title: Green Bonds for Climate Finance
learn.intro: Green bonds channel private capital into renewable projects, accelerating the transition to clean energy. They provide transparent, ESG‑aligned funding, ensuring investors support climate solutions while developers secure low‑cost, long‑term financing.
learn.c1_text: Green bonds are debt securities earmarked for projects that deliver environmental benefits. Think of them as a green piggy bank: investors deposit money, and the proceeds fund solar farms, wind turbines, or battery storage, with returns linked to project performance.
learn.c2_text: Issuers pledge funds to renewable projects, audited by third parties. A $1 billion green bond can finance 200 MW of solar, illustrating how capital flows directly into low‑carbon infrastructure.
learn.c3_text: India issued its first green bond in 2019, raising $1.3 billion for solar and wind. The 2024 bond cycle aims to double that to $3 billion, supporting the country’s 450 GW renewable target.
learn.stat1: Global green bond issuance hit $269 bn in 2023
learn.stat2: India’s green bond market grew 200% from 2019 to 2023
learn.stat3: Green bonds financed 12 GW of renewable capacity worldwide in 2023
learn.stat4: Indian issuers plan $3 bn green bonds in 2024

learn.title: Flow Batteries for Grid Storage
learn.intro: Flow batteries store energy in liquid electrolytes, allowing unlimited capacity scaling and long cycle life. They are ideal for balancing variable renewables, providing grid stability, and extending the lifespan of intermittent wind and solar assets.
learn.c1_text: Two tanks hold electrolytes; during charging, ions move through a membrane, storing energy. Think of it like two water reservoirs exchanging water—capacity grows by adding more tanks, not by changing the cell.
learn.c2_text: Vanadium flow batteries offer high energy density (~30 Wh/l) and 10‑year lifespan, while iron‑based systems are cheaper (~$200/kWh) but lower density (~15 Wh/l), suitable for large‑scale grid storage.
learn.c3_text: India’s 2026 plan includes 500 MW of flow battery storage to support the 280 GW solar target, reducing curtailment by 20% and enhancing grid resilience.
learn.stat1: Global flow battery market valued at $2.5 bn in 2023
learn.stat4: India aims for 500 MW flow battery capacity by 2026

learn.title: Hydrogen Fuel Cells in Vehicles
learn.intro: Hydrogen fuel cells convert chemical energy directly into electricity, producing only water vapor. They enable zero‑emission vehicles with quick refueling and long ranges, bridging the gap where batteries face weight or charging time constraints.
learn.c1_text: Protons move through a membrane while electrons travel via an external circuit, generating power. Imagine a tiny generator that runs on water—hydrogen is the fuel, the membrane the filter, electrons the electricity.
learn.c2_text: A 1‑kW fuel cell can power a car at 100 km/h. Refueling takes 3 minutes, and 1 kg of H₂ yields 33 kWh, giving ranges of 500 km—comparable to battery EVs but with faster refueling.
learn.c3_text: India’s 2025 target is 10 000 fuel‑cell buses, supported by a $50 bn subsidy, aiming to cut urban CO₂ by 30 Mt annually, illustrating policy‑driven deployment.
learn.stat1: Global fuel cell vehicle sales hit 10 000 units in 2023
learn.stat3: Fuel cell vehicles can refuel in 3 minutes

learn.title: India Green Hydrogen Mission
learn.intro: India’s National Green Hydrogen Mission aims to produce 5 Mt of green hydrogen by 2030, supporting industrial decarbonization and creating a domestic supply chain. It leverages solar and wind power to electrolyze water, providing clean fuel for transport and power generation.
learn.c1_text: Produce 5 Mt of green hydrogen, reduce cost to $1.5/kg, and build 10 GW electrolyzer capacity—similar to scaling a city’s electricity grid to meet a new energy demand.
learn.c2_text: Subsidies cover 30% of electrolyzer cost, tax breaks, and a green hydrogen credit market, encouraging private investment and ensuring affordability for industry.
learn.c3_text: India will partner with 20 state governments, deploying 1 GW electrolyzers by 2025 and scaling to 10 GW by 2030, showcasing a phased, collaborative approach.
learn.stat1: India’s green hydrogen target: 5 Mt by 2030

learn.title: Blue vs Green vs Grey Hydrogen
learn.intro: Understanding hydrogen colors clarifies their environmental impact. Grey hydrogen is fossil‑fuel based, blue captures and stores CO₂, and green uses renewable electricity for electrolysis. Choosing the right type is key to achieving true decarbonization in industry and transport.
learn.c1_text: Produced via steam methane reforming, grey hydrogen emits ~9 t CO₂ per 1 Mt—like burning coal in a factory, it’s cheap but polluting.
learn.c2_text: Blue hydrogen adds carbon capture and storage, reducing emissions by ~90%. It’s like turning a busy highway into a closed loop, keeping CO₂ out of the atmosphere.
learn.c3_text: Generated by electrolyzing water with renewables, green hydrogen emits virtually zero CO₂, offering a clean fuel for India’s 2030 decarbonization goals.
learn.stat1: Grey hydrogen accounts for 95% of global production
learn.stat2: Blue hydrogen could cut CO₂ by 90% if CCS is effective
learn.stat4: India plans 5 Mt green hydrogen by 2030
